constructive_proposals: natural forces can produce miraculous effects without supernatural intervention
healing works may be performed through natural, good influences rather than evil powers
the power of imagination and mental influence can affect the body without physical agents
certain natural substances (e.g., lodestone) have hidden powers that explain extraordinary phenomena

scapegoat.name: witches
attributes: stir up and confound the elements
distract minds of men, driving them to madness, insane hatred, and inordinate lusts
work through the aid of the devil
scapegoat.justification: The text identifies witches as agents who invoke evil forces (the devil) to produce unnatural effects like storms, mental derangement, and bodily harm, thereby casting them as the primary source of moral corruption and supernatural disruption.

moral_justification: The text argues that extraordinary or harmful events are not caused by witchcraft or demonic power but by natural forces or the imagination, and that attributing such effects to witches unjustly absolves natural causes and misrepresents moral responsibility.
summary: This passage presents a medieval philosophical and theological argument that challenges the notion of witchcraft as a supernatural force, instead attributing bodily changes, miracles, and extraordinary events to natural powers, mental influence, or hidden physical properties. While it deconstructs the idea of witches as agents of evil through rational and religious reasoning, it also offers constructive proposals about the mind-body connection, natural causation, and legitimate healing practices, resulting in a mixed rhetorical mode with moderate emotional intensity.
